story:
In a quiet forest, an ancient apple tree narrates as keeper of the woods and guardian of the spirits of women buried at its roots. The tree senses him before he appears—the boy whose laughter always curdles into cruelty. He arrives with a new girl on his arm, a picnic blanket, and a charming smile. To strangers they look perfect. He lays out his feast, and when the girl notices the tree’s bright red apples, he plucks one down with a flourish. He pretends to offer it, only to snatch it back and take the first bite, a small, petty ritual that announces the harm to come.

Evening drifts by with fragile glasses, easy talk, and practiced tenderness. Then, as always, his hands circle a trusting neck. The tree, rooted and unable to intervene, witnesses another life snuffed out. He takes a gruesome trophy, as he always does, then digs the grave among the others at the tree’s base. The songbirds fall silent. When the girl’s spirit wakes, she is bound to her burial, as all the others are, until something like closure comes. The tree comforts her as best it can. But unlike the others—sorrowful and stunned—she is incandescent with purpose.

Winter passes. In her fierce resolve, the last victim does what none before her could: she dances through the branches, upsetting blossoms until only one survives—one perfect-looking apple that she saturates with all the grief and fury the tree has held. Inside, it is ruin, made poison by the memory of what was done to them.

Spring ripens into summer. He returns as if pulled by habit or vanity, this time with a new girl whose trust is quickly caught in his familiar spell. They see the single apple, hanging just within reach. The pattern repeats: a teasing chase around the trunk, a stolen first bite, a smug smile. But the script breaks. The bitten apple fights back. Pain seizes him. He claws at his throat, gasping, and collapses as the forest watches. The girl, terrified, flees. The tree does not rejoice, but a profound stillness settles: the cycle has ended.

One by one, the spirits are released, their long grief lightening at last. The last victim lingers a moment, as if nodding to the tree and the fallen man, then she too is free. Yet the boy’s presence does not pass on. When scavengers and weather, over many quiet days, return his body at last to the roots he used to hide his crimes, his harm lingers where he inflicted it. As long as the tree stands, it holds him there, the way it held the sorrow of the women he wronged, not out of malice but out of a grave sort of justice.

With dawn, the forest restores its quiet. Birds sing again. Leaves stir in a clean breeze. The tree remains—wiser, wearier, still a witness—and the grove breathes easier, the old pain finally answered, the living and the lost at peace.
